A strong upper air trough developed north of the islands and brought heavy rain and flash flooding to parts of the island chain. Some properties on Oahu were damaged by the inundation, while residents on Maui saw road closures because of the precipitation. No serious injuries were reported. The cost of damages was not available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gulches in the Haiku and Makawao areas of East Maui overflowed due to heavy rainfall. Low water crossings became flooded, so county officials had to close roadways for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a surface low remaining north and northeast of the state, and a surface trough to the east; heavy downpours occurred in parts of Maui and the Big Island as deep tropical moisture made its way northward. The episode resulted in a flash flooding event in the Puna District on the Big Island. No significant injuries were reported. The cost of any damages was not available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Puna District in windward Big Island led to the closure of Pohaku Drive at the 39th Avenue intersection. The normally dry Waipahoehoe Stream overflowed and forced the closing of the roadway in Orchidlands Estates.

Southwest to west winds increased to more than 80 mph over the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island. No significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A surface trough just east of the island chain, in combination with high pressure north of the state, induced strong north winds across the area. Damages from the winds occurred on the Big Island and Oahu, with one individual injured on Oahu as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tin roof of a building was blown off at the Kona Airport in the leeward part of the Big Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough just east of the island chain, in combination with high pressure north of the state, induced strong north winds across the area. Damages from the winds occurred on the Big Island and Oahu, with one individual injured on Oahu as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six trees were downed near Hilo in the windward part of the Big Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough just east of the island chain, in combination with high pressure north of the state, induced strong north winds across the area. Damages from the winds occurred on the Big Island and Oahu, with one individual injured on Oahu as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Oahu, a house lost its roof at the corner of Kilauea and Ahuawa Loop in Kahala due to strong winds. An 89-year-old man was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blaze charred about 200 acres of dry brush near Wailea in leeward East Maui. Around 100 people had to evacuate the area, including guests at a luxury hotel. Firefighters struggled for a time with the blaze because of breezy, swirling winds. No injuries were reported, and the cost of any damages was not available. The cause of the fire was not known.
Dry conditions began to increase over parts of the Big Island and Maui in January. The D2 category of severe drought took hold in these areas.

The combination of wind waves and an incoming swell from the northeast generated surf of 8 to 15 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island. No serious injuries or property damage were reported, though Bayfront Highway  along Hilo Harbor on the Big Island was closed for a time on the 29th because of debris and water on the roadway from the high surf. Coconut Island and Liliuokalani Park near Banyan Drive were also closed on the 29th in the Hilo area.

A strong upper air trough developed north of the islands and brought heavy rain and flash flooding to parts of the island chain. Some properties on Oahu were damaged by the inundation, while residents on Maui saw road closures because of the precipitation. No serious injuries were reported. The cost of damages was not available.

A swell from a strong low far northeast of the islands generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the north-facing, and 8 to 15 feet along the east-facing, shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island. No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State caused surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.  One individual surfer, a male in his 50's, died at a hospital in Maui after being taken from the water off northwest West Maui.  He had been unresponsive when brought to shore by fellow surfers.  No other serious injuries or fatalities were reported.  There were no reports of significant property damage.

Portions of the Big Island and Maui remained in severe drought, or the D2 category on the Drought Monitor, for part of February. However, rainfall was sufficient for both areas to be classified at no worse than the D1 category of moderate drought by the end of the month.

With a surface low remaining north and northeast of the state, and a surface trough to the east; heavy downpours occurred in parts of Maui and the Big Island as deep tropical moisture made its way northward. The episode resulted in a flash flooding event in the Puna District on the Big Island. No significant injuries were reported. The cost of any damages was not available.
Winds from the south to southwest were sustained above 60 mph, with gusts as high as 77 mph. No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A blaze scorched around 525 acres of dry brush in rough terrain on Oahu.  The fire started upslope of Kaukonahua Road north of Schofield Range near Wahiawa.  It was difficult to contain because of the mountainous location of the blaze.  No vegetation or structures or were threatened, and no injuries were reported.  The cause of the fire was not known.

A powerful storm low moved to a position just north and northeast of Hawaii and wreaked havoc on the islands. The system brought fierce winds to most areas, with lower level winds exceeding 50-knot gusts on Kauai at Port Allen and Barking Sands; and enhanced surf, especially along north- and west-facing shores. Surf heights in some locales reached above 50 feet for north-facing shores, and above 30 feet for west-facing shores. And the very strong winds at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island, and the summit of Haleakala on Maui, produced blizzard-type conditions for a time as the feature affected the region. No significant injuries were reported. The costs of damages, public and private, were not available.

Surf heights in some locales reached above 50 feet for north-facing shores, and above 30 feet for west-facing shores. And the very strong winds at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island, and the summit of Haleakala on Maui, produced blizzard-type conditions for a time as the feature affected the region. No significant injuries were reported. The costs of damages, public and private, were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Oahu, a downed utility pole along Kunia Road near Hawaii Country Club closed both lanes of traffic for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of wind waves and an incoming swell from the northeast generated surf of 8 to 12 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island. No serious injuries or property damage were reported. This is a continuation of an episode that began toward the end of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blaze charred more than 110 acres of brush in the US Army's Pohakuloa Training Area on the slopes of Mauna Kea on the Big Island. The fire was about two miles north of the Daniel K. Inouye Highway and on the Kona (west) side of Mauna Kea Access Road. No significant property damage or injuries were reported. The cause of the blaze was unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly SW to W winds were sustained anywhere from 60 to 135 mph, with gusts as high as 161 mph, at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island. Winds were somewhat less at the summit of Haleakala on Maui. Blizzard-type conditions occurred from time to time with blowing snow and freezing precipitation, especially on Haleakala above 6000 feet. As an aside, snow at that elevation on Maui, 6000 feet, is extremely rare.

There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

And the very strong winds at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island, and the summit of Haleakala on Maui, produced blizzard-type conditions for a time as the feature affected the region. No significant injuries were reported. The costs of damages, public and private, were not available.

Bands of showers riding on breezy trade winds affected windward parts of the Valley Isle of Maui.  The precipitation produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways,  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

With a light background wind flow across the area, convergent sea breezes led to heavy showers over central and western sections of Oahu.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

Dry vegetation and a breezy wind flow contributed to the ignition and spreading of a nearly 1000-acre blaze over leeward West Maui.  It started in Kahana above the Kapalua Airport and spread along the ridge line in that section of the West Maui Mountains.  The fire closed the airport for a time because of a power outage, and flights were diverted to Kahului.  Traffic in the area was also affected from time to time, and some residents were asked to evacuate as a precaution as the flames threatened to spread farther afield.  However, no significant injuries were reported.  The costs of any damages were not available.  The cause of the blaze was not provided.

A wind-swept fire began upslope from Maalaea in Maui, in the West Maui Mountains, that eventually consumed around 4600 acres of dry brush.  The flames forced the evacuation of more than 450 residents along Maalaea Bay Road as the blaze moved close to homes in the area.  The fire also forced the closure of streets and highways in parts of West and central Maui.  However, in the end, there were no significant injuries or structural damages.  The cause of the blaze was under investigation.

A pair of brush fires combined to create a major hazardous episode over a portion of the Valley Isle of Maui.  The blaze blackened around 9200 acres of old, dry sugarcane plantation fields and unmanaged grasslands in the isle's Central Valley.  The fire was first reported in the later morning on July 11th, south of Kahului and south of the intersection between Kuihelani Highway and Waiko Road.  Many residents were evacuated from the area, roads were closed, power was lost for a time, and many flights from the Kahului Airport were cancelled.  The Maui Humane Society also had to evacuate the animals at its shelter.||The fire was fully contained by the morning of the 15th.  No significant injuries were reported.  The cost of damages was not available, and the cause of the blaze was not known.

Swells generated by Tropical Cyclones Erick and Flossie produced surf of 8 to 12 feet, with some sets in the 15 to 20-foot range, along east and/or south facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

With deep tropical moisture moving north from Tropical Storm Erick, heavy showers developed over east and southeast portions of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  However, no significant property damage or injuries were reported.

With remnant moisture over the western part of the Aloha State (from a departing Tropical Depression Erick southwest of the state), an upper level disturbance triggered showers and thunderstorms over portions of Kauai.  The precipitation generated flash flooding in the northeast section of the isle, in Anahola and Kapaa.  There were no reports of serious injuries, however.  The costs of any damages were not available.

Two fires burned about 200 acres of dry brush in leeward Oahu in Kunia.  Five individuals were asked to  leave their homes for a time as firefighters contained the combined blaze. Three storage containers were damaged in the Kunia Farm lots.  However, no injuries were reported.  The cost of damages was not available.  The cause of the fires was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with good upper level support pushed its way through the island chain around the Christmas holiday, bringing periods of heavy precipitation, isolated thunderstorms, and strong to high winds.  The weather system caused a flash flood and felled trees and power poles, especially on the Garden Isle of Kauai.  No significant injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large swell shifting from the north to northeast generated surf of 6 to 12 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island. The surf was high enough on the Big Island on the 7th that an over-wash occurred along Bayfront Highway in Hilo that left debris on the roadway, and a foot of standing water was observed on Coconut Island. Otherwise, no serious property damage or injuries were reported.

With a front approaching Kauai from the northwest, a surface trough near the Big Island, and a slow-moving upper level disturbance; heavy showers and thunderstorms formed as the features acted on abundant moisture.  Rainfall affected most of the isles, with flash flooding occurring in Kauai and on the Big Island.  However, no serious injuries were reported.  Minor property damage occurred on Oahu as a thunderstorm with gusty winds felled a tree west of Pearl Harbor in the late evening of the 18th, and a car was damaged by a falling branch and a tree uprooted in Waipio; and there were minor power outages during the same time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kuhio highway was closed near the Hanalei Bridge due to water overflowing the Hanalei River.  A bit later, Kuhio Highway became impassable as the Wainiha River overflowed its banks at the Twin Bridges in Wainiha.  A woman was swept away by the flood waters in that area, but she was rescued by the Kauai Fire Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai. No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet, with occasional higher sets, along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  Strengthening trade winds caused a short-period swell that produced surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

With a front approaching Kauai from the northwest, a surface trough near the Big Island, and a slow-moving upper level disturbance; heavy showers and thunderstorms formed as the features acted on abundant moisture.  Rainfall affected most of the isles, with flash flooding occurring in Kauai and on the Big Island.  However, no serious injuries were reported.  Minor property damage occurred on Oahu as a thunderstorm with gusty winds felled a tree west of Pearl Harbor in the late evening of the 18th, and a car was damaged by a falling branch and a tree uprooted in Waipio; and there were minor power outages during the same time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures on social media showed a medium-sized tree uprooted and several large branches down, including one on top of a car which shattered the back glass.  This was in Waipio in central Oahu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet, with occasional higher sets, along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  Strengthening trade winds caused a short-period swell that produced surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A moist trade wind flow across the island chain helped trigger afternoon downpours in the eastern part of East Maui.  The rainfall caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.
